segments.s224-g25: This is a verbless nominal clause that links two noun-phrases to a predicate: 'سر نیزه و نام من' (the spear's tip and my name) is equated with 'مرگ'. The final word 'تست' is an archaic/poetic copular element here; in classical Persian it functions like 'is/are for you' (addressing the addressee), so the whole segment means 'the spear's point and my name are Death (for you)'. Note the coordinate structure (X و Y) before the predicate.
segments.s224-g26: A full verbal clause: 'سرت را' marks 'your head' as the definite object (را). 'بباید' is an older/poetic modal verb meaning 'must/ought' (present/future sense). 'ز تن' is the poetic preposition 'from the body', and 'دست شست' is a two-word predicate where 'شست' is the past tense 'washed' (3rd person) and 'دست' is its subject 'the hand'. Together: 'your head must, from the body, the hand washed' → 'your head must be (or will be) washed from your body by the hand.'
segments.s224-g27: 'بیامد همانگاه' is a temporal clause meaning 'he came/arrived immediately' or 'immediately came'. 'چنگش' is a possessive compound (çang + -esh) meaning 'his grip/claw/hold' (here 'his grasp' or 'his hand'), and 'چو باد' is a simile 'like the wind'. Word order is subject-verb/time-adverb then the compared noun: 'his grasp came immediately like the wind.'
segments.s224-g28: This clause is compressed and poetic. 'دو زاغ' is the direct object or accusative phrase 'two rooks/crows'. 'کمان را' uses the object marker را on 'the bow' (here topicalized/definite). The verbal phrase 'بزه بر نهاد' has 'نهاد' (past of نهادن, 'placed/put') as the main verb; 'بر' = 'on/against'. 'بزه' in classical usage is unusual here (likely a noun 'a thing placed' or a specialized term); the sequence therefore reads literally 'he placed two rooks upon the bow.' The exact poetic image (two crows on the bow) is metaphorical.
segments.s224-g29: 'کمان جفا پیشه' is a genitive-adjective phrase where 'جفا پیشه' is a compound adjective meaning 'practicing cruelty' or 'given to cruelty' attached to 'کمان' (the bow). 'چون ابر بود' is a simile 'was like a cloud', with 'بود' the past copula 'was'. So the line says 'the bow, practicing cruelty, was like a cloud.'
segments.s224-g30: This is a second clause continuing the description: 'هم آورد' = 'also brought' or 'also carried'. 'با جوشن و گبر بود' attaches 'با' ('with') to 'جوشن' (coat of mail/armor) and links with 'گبر' (a Zoroastrian/infidel) by 'و'. The final 'بود' is again the past copula. Literally: 'It also bore (or came) with armor and was a gabr' — meaning the bow (or the warrior using it) was equipped with armor and was an infidel in the poet's portrayal.
segments.s224-g31: 'سپر بر سرآورد' is a light-verb construction: 'سپر' (shield) + 'بر' ('upon') + 'سرآورد' (literally 'put to the head' or 'raised to the head'). In context it means 'he raised the shield over his head.' 'رستم' is the subject placed after the verb (poetic word order). 'چو دید' ('when he saw') introduces the subordinate clause; 'چو' = 'when' and 'دید' = 'he saw'.
segments.s224-g32: A subordinate content clause introduced by 'که' (= 'that'/'because'/'who'). 'تیرش' = 'his arrow' (possessive), 'زره را' marks 'the armor' as definite object, and the verbal complex 'بخواهد برید' uses the present/subjunctive/modal 'بخواهد' (here 'would/attempt/seek to') + 'برید' (infinitive/past-form of بریدن 'to cut/cleave' — poetic compression). The overall meaning is 'that his arrow would try to cut/pierce the armor.' Poetic word order places object before verb.
segments.s224-g33: بدو (a prepositional pronoun) + گفت (past verb) = 'said to him'; classical word order allows the pronoun before the verb. باش‌ای is the imperative باش 'be' with the vocative particle ای attached: 'Be, O ...'. سوار دلیر is a vocative noun phrase 'O brave rider', with the adjective دلیر after the noun.
segments.s224-g34: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/for'); اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. سرت is the possessor + noun 'your head' as the grammatical subject. گردد is the present/subjunctive form of گشت/گردیدن meaning 'become' (here a hortative/wish or future-like sense: 'may become'/'will become'). از marks cause/relationship ('of/from'), رزم is the noun 'battle', and سیر is the predicate noun 'sated' (so 'سرت گردد از رزم سیر' = 'may your head become sated from/of battle').
segments.s224-g35: نگه کرد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'he looked/fixed his gaze' or 'held fast'; نگه(s) is the noun/gaze and کرد is the light/past verb. چنگش is 'his cheng/hand/grip' (possessive -ش) and functions as the object. بران is an archaic locative/adpositional particle 'on/at that', and پیلتن is a place-name or specific referent.
segments.s224-g36: ببالای is the prepositional compound 'up on/high upon'. سرو is the noun 'cypress' followed by the epithet سهی (adjective after noun, classical order) meaning 'dark/tall/poetic epithet'. بر چمن is a locative phrase 'on the meadow' (بر = 'on', چمن = 'meadow').
segments.s224-g37: بد is a predicative adjective placed before the noun for emphasis ('bad/ill was...'). آن اسپ = 'that steed' (اسپ is an older/frequent epic word for 'horse'). در زیر یک لخت کوه is a locative: در زیر = 'under/beneath', یک لخت کوه = 'a bare/stripped part of the mountain' (لخت modifying کوه).
segments.s224-g38: نیامد is the negative past/imperfect 'did not come' and here carries the sense 'would not advance/come forward'. همی is a classical continuous/emphatic preverbal particle (marks iterative/ongoing aspect). از + کشیدن marks the cause ('from pulling/hauling'); کشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'to draw/pull', and ستوه is an adjective 'weary', yielding 'did not come, being weary from pulling.'
segments.s224-g39: بدل here is an adverb/adverbial phrase 'in return' or 'in reply' modifying گفت. چنگش is again the possessive 'his grip' personified as the speaker (a poetic device). که introduces the reported content 'that/that now ...', اکنون = 'now', and گریز is a nominal predicate 'flight/escape'.
segments.s224-g40: به از is a classical comparative idiom meaning 'better than' (structure: 'X به از Y' = 'X is better than Y'). با تن خویش = 'with one's own body' (تن = 'body'; خوی + ش forms the reflexive/possessive 'خویش' = 'one's own'). کردن + ستیز is the verbalization 'to make/perform strife' = 'to fight/contend'. Put together: 'گریز به از با تن خویش کردن ستیز' = 'flight is better than fighting with one's own body.'
segments.s224-g41: برانگیخت is past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular ('he/it roused/raised'); it begins the clause. آن بارکش را is the definite object (آن = 'that', بارکش = 'beast of burden') marked by the object marker را. ز جای is a prepositional phrase using poetic ز = 'from' + جای = 'place' and completes the verb's idea ('roused ... from its place').
segments.s224-g42: سوی is the preposition 'toward'; لشکر خویشتن is the object of that direction ('his own army'), with خویشتن adding emphatic/reflective 'own'. کرد رای is a light‑verb construction: کرد ('made/did') + رای ('plan/decision'), so here it means 'resolved/steered/decided on a course' — i.e. he directed himself (or his action) toward his army.
segments.s224-g43: This is a stacked adjectival/noun phrase describing the agent: بکردار ('of/with a manner' or 'having the quality of') + آتش ('fire', used metaphorically as 'fiery') + دلاور ('valiant') + سوار ('rider/horseman'). The words form a compact epithet 'the fiery‑mannered valiant rider.'
segments.s224-g44: برانگیخت is again the past 3rd‑person 'he roused'; رخش is the direct object 'his steed' (word order: verb then object is allowed in classical verse). از پس = 'from/behind' (از + پس) gives the spatial relation, and نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned/famed' modifying رخش.
segments.s224-g45: همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately/at once'; رستم is the subject; رسید is the past 3rd‑person 'arrived/reached'; اندروی is a locative adverb/phrase here meaning 'into the inner place' or 'into their midst' (it marks where he arrived).
segments.s224-g46: همه دشت = 'the whole/plain entire plain' (همه as 'whole, entire' modifying دشت). زیشان is a pronominal genitive 'of them / among them' linking the plain to the people; پر از is the phrase 'full of' and گفت و گوی is the compound 'conversation/discourse' (گفت and گوی together form گفت‌وگو). Word order reflects classical Persian: determiner/adjective + noun + genitive + predicate.
segments.s224-g47: دم here functions temporally as 'at the moment' (rather than 'tail'); اسپ ناپاک is 'the impure/unclean steed' (noun + adjective); چنگش is a possessive form ('his/its grip, claw, or hold' — the suffix -ش = 'his/its') and گرفت is the past 3rd‑person 'seized/took'. Together: 'At that moment the unclean steed seized his hold.'
segments.s224-g48: دو لشکر is the subject 'two armies'; بدو is the short prepositional/pronominal form 'to him/at him'; مانده is the participial/past form 'remained/stayed' indicating their state; اندر شگفت is an adverbial phrase 'in wonder/astonishment'. The clause means 'both armies remained astonished at him.'
segments.s224-g1: ازان (poetic compound of از + آن) is a prepositional phrase ‘from that/there’; دشت is its noun complement ‘plain’. چنگش is چنگ + possessive -ش ‘his grip/hold’ (here a bodily or equipment-related noun); برانگیخت is a past transitive verb ‘roused/urged on/raised’. اسپ (steed) is the object affected by برانگیخت. Word order is typical older Persian: prepositional phrase + possessor + verb + patient.
segments.s224-g2: همی is a verbal particle marking continuous or iterative aspect in older Persian (‘kept on / was’); رفت is the past tense of رفتن ‘went/rode’. برسان is an infinitival/causative form ‘to bring/deliver (to)’, and the following proper name آذرگشسپ is the intended recipient of whatever is being brought. The phrase means he rode off in order to bring something to Azar-gashasp (the direct object is implicit or understood from context).
segments.s224-g3: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’; نزدیک here functions adverbially ‘near/approaching’; ایرانیان is the plural noun ‘the Iranians’ (object of approach); شد is a past auxiliary/verb meaning ‘became / came to be’ (i.e. ‘drew near’); بجنگ is an adverbial/infinitival phrase ‘to battle / for battle’ (literally ‘into fight’). Together: ‘when he drew near the Iranians to battle.’
segments.s224-g4: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’; here ترکش is written as ترک + possessive ش (see notes) and means ‘his quiver/arrow-stock’ (the lemma list split it). ش is the 3rd-person possessive clitic ‘his’. برآورد is an older/periphrastic past meaning ‘brought forth / drew out / let fly’ (here ‘drew out’ or ‘sent’). تیر خدنگ is a noun phrase where تیر ‘arrow’ is qualified by خدنگ ‘dagger-like / short, sharp weapon’ — ‘a dagger-like arrow’. Word order is poetic: from + (quiver)-his + verb + [arrow descriptor].
segments.s224-g5: چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so’ that frames reported speech; گفت here functions as the verb ‘he said’ (the lemma-list entry given is dialog/‘saying’). کین in classical usage can be a demonstrative ‘this/that’ or mean ‘vengeance’; in this line it introduces the clause ‘this place of battle…’. جای جنگ is a genitive construction ‘place of battle’ (جای + جنگ). منست is an archaic contraction corresponding to ‘is mine / is for me’ (classical ‘من است’ or an idiomatic predicate).
segments.s224-g6: سر نامداران is a genitive phrase: سر ‘heads’ (literal or metonymic) + نامداران ‘the renowned/illustrious ones’. بچنگ is a prefixed prepositional/adverbial form meaning ‘into the grasp / in (my) grip’ (ب + چنگ). منست repeats the archaic predicate ‘is mine / belongs to me’. The whole clause is a boast: the heads of the famous lie in my grasp.
segments.s224-g7: کجا رفت is the interrogative ‘where did (he) go?’ (کجا + past رفتن). آن مرد is ‘that man’. کاموس گیر is a compound epithet: کاموس (here a name or descriptive noun; the precise referent is context-dependent) + گیر ‘catcher/seizer’ (agent noun). The phrase asks the whereabouts of that man, described by this epithet.
segments.s224-g8: که is a relative pronoun ‘who/that’ linking to the man; گاهی is an adverb ‘sometimes’; کمند is ‘lasso/snare’ and افگند is the past ‘threw/cast’ (i.e. ‘cast a noose’); گاه (here) again ‘at times/occasionally’; تیر is ‘arrow’. The clause enumerates his habitual actions: sometimes he cast a lasso, at other times an arrow.
segments.s224-g57: همی بود is an archaic/perfective past-continuous construction (همی + بود) — here meaning 'was standing' or 'stood repeatedly/continually'; the verb precedes the subject (بود رستم) which is common in poetic word order. میان دو صف is a prepositional phrase 'between two ranks/lines' following the subject.
segments.s224-g58: گرفته is a perfect-participle/relative participle 'having taken' or 'holding' that modifies Rostam; یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one/a single'; خشت is the noun 'brick (mudbrick)'; رخشان is an adjective 'shining, radiant' describing the brick; بکف is a locative phrase 'in (his) hand' (ب + کف). The order (noun + adjective, participle before its noun phrase) reflects classical/poetic Persian syntax.
segments.s224-g59: وزان is a fused prepositional element 'from that' (referring to the previous sight); روی here means 'face' or 'appearance/sight' that provoked the reaction; خاقان is the subject 'the Khaghan (ruler)'; غمی گشت is a verbal phrase 'became grief/was filled with grief' where گشت is past 'became'; سخت modifies گشت to mean 'severely/deeply' (degree adverb).
segments.s224-g60: برآشفت is a past/preterite verb meaning 'became upset/agitated' (literally 'rose up against/was enraged'); با introduces the cause 'by/with'; گردش چرخ و بخت is a compound causal phrase 'the turning (گردش) of the wheel (چرخ) and fate/destiny (بخت)' — here چرخ and بخت together personify cosmic fortune. The whole phrase explains the agitation as caused by the revolutions of fate.
segments.s224-g61: بهومان is the indirect object 'to Humān' (addressed person); چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech that follows; گفت is the verb 'said' (poetic placement has the verb near the adverb); خاقان چین is the agent 'the Khaghan of China' (title + origin). The line uses inverted/compact word order typical of epic diction: 'بهومان چنین گفت خاقان چین' = 'the Chinese Khaghan said thus to Humān.'
segments.s224-g62: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; تنگست is an old fused form of تنگ است 'is narrow/tight' (one word in classical verse); بر ما is a dative-like construction 'upon us/for us'; زمان و زمین 'time and earth' are the two nouns governed by تنگست, meaning here 'our time and our land (or the present age and the world) are constrained/pressing.'
segments.s224-g63: مران نامور پهلوان is an appositive noun phrase 'Maran, the renowned champion'; را marks that phrase as the definite object/topic; تو here indicates the person to whom the matter is assigned ('to you' — classical Persian often places the indirect object after را); نام in this construction relates to being 'named/assigned' — the line therefore says that Maran the champion has been named/assigned to you.
segments.s224-g64: شوی is the (archaic/poetic) verbal form 'you are made/it has been made to you' (from شدن; here indicating assignment/appointment). بازجویی is a noun 'inquiry/interrogation' that states the purpose; فرستی is a 2nd-person singular verbal form used optatively/imperatively 'send' (thou send), and پیام is the direct object 'a message' — together فرستی پیام بازجویی = 'send a message for inquiry' / 'dispatch a messenger to ask.'
segments.s224-g65: بدو (= be-dō) is a prepositional phrase ‘to him/unto him’ placed before the reporting verb for emphasis; گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’ introducing reported speech; که is the complementizer ‘that’ (introducing the content of what Hūmān says); سندان is the predicate-noun ‘anvil’; نیم here is an archaic negative-copula meaning ‘I am not’ (repeated after the second nominal).
segments.s224-g66: برزم is a locative/adverbial specifying the battlefield/at war (archaic placement before the clause); اندرون = ‘inside/within’; پیل = ‘elephant’; دندان = ‘tooth/tusk’ used as the predicate-noun; نیم again is the archaic 1st‑person negative (‘I am not’), so the whole clause repeats the negative predicate: ‘nor am I the inner tusk of an elephant.’
segments.s224-g67: بگیتی is an adverbial locative ‘in the world / among men’; چو is the comparative particle ‘like/as’; کاموس is a proper name (the hero being compared); جنگی is a predicative adjective/noun ‘warlike / a warrior’; نبود is the past negative copula ‘there was not / was not.’
segments.s224-g68: چنو (= چنان) is an intensifying demonstrative ‘such/so’; رزم‌خواه is a noun ‘war‑seeking / warrior’; و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’; درنگی is a noun ‘a pause / hesitation’; نبود again is the past negative ‘there was not.’ The two coordinated nominal predicates mean ‘there was neither such a warrior nor any hesitation.’
segments.s224-g69: بخم is an adverbial/archaic adverb meaning something like ‘suddenly/secretly/from the flank’; کمندش is ‘his snare/lasso’ (کمند + enclitic -ش ‘his/its’); گرفت is the past tense verb ‘seized/took’; این سوار (the subject) follows the verb for emphatic word order ‘this rider.’ The clause is SOV-typical with an initial adverb and a possessive object.
segments.s224-g70: تو is the vocative/2nd‑person address ‘you’; این گرد را is the definite object (این + گرد with را marking it) — here گرد denotes the group/band/host (an older sense); خوار is an adjective ‘despised/base’; مایه is a noun ‘cause/substance/value’ (‘a thing of value’ or here ‘a cause/reason’); مدار is an imperative negative formation here meaning ‘do not make/consider’ (archaic usage). Grammatically, را marks the object that the speaker instructs the addressee not to demean.
segments.s224-g71: شوم is an optative/subjunctive first‑person form ‘may I become / may it happen’ (expressing a wish or ill‑omen); تا in this construction introduces the content/result (‘so that / that’ or nearly ‘to see / such that’); چه is interrogative ‘what’; خواهد is the future/optative auxiliary ‘will/wishes/shall’; جهان‌آفرین is the subject ‘the world‑creator / the Creator’ — the whole clause asks rhetorically what the Creator intends to do.
segments.s224-g72: که is the subordinating/completive particle ‘that’; پیروز is adjectival/nominative ‘victorious’; گردد is the subjunctive/potential ‘becomes / may become’ (here expressing who will prevail); بدین is the compound prepositional sense ‘in/to this’ (written here split as ب + این in the data); دشت is the noun ‘plain/field’; کین is a genitive attribute ‘of vengeance/hatred’ — together: ‘who will be victorious in this plain of vengeance.’
segments.s224-g49: زمانی is a temporal adverb (‘for a while’) at the clause start. همی داشت is the past-continuous construction (همی + داشت) marking ongoing action in the past. تا introduces the boundary clause meaning ‘until’, and شد غمی is a short, poetic clause where شد (past of شدن) means ‘befell’/‘happened’ and غمی is the affected noun ‘a grief’ (word order here is elevated/poetic: verb + noun).
segments.s224-g50: ز (poetic از) + بالا forms the prepositional phrase ‘from/on high’ that precedes the verb. بزد is simple past (3rd sg.) ‘he struck/smote’. خویشتن is the emphatic reflexive ‘himself’ functioning as the direct object, and بر + زمی expresses direction/place ‘upon the earth/ground’ (بر = ‘onto/upon’, زمی = ‘ground/earth’). The phrase order (prepositional phrase — verb — object — goal) reflects poetic word order.
segments.s224-g51: بیفتاد is past tense ‘fell’ (3rd sg.). ز is the poetic form of از ‘from’, so زو = از او ‘from him’. The coordinating و links clauses (‘and’). ترگ is the noun ‘shred/rag’ (object of بیفتاد). The second و links the exclamation that follows; زنهار is an interjection/cry (here ‘zanehâr’ ≈ ‘mercy/for pity’s sake’) and خواست is past tense ‘he cried/asked’ (here ‘he uttered’ the exclamation).
segments.s224-g52: تهمتن is an epithet/name (Tahmētan, here agent). ورا is the enclitic/old object-form meaning ‘to him’/‘him’ (ورا = او را). کرد is the simple past ‘did/made’ used here as the main verb (‘caused/made/forced’). با + خاک indicates the instrument or accompaniment ‘with the earth/onto the ground’, and راست (here) modifies the manner as ‘straight/direct’ (‘straight to the ground’). Note the compact, high-register phrasing common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s224-g53: همانگاه is an adverb meaning ‘immediately/at that moment’. کردش is کرد + enclitic -ش (او را) = ‘he did to him’ (here ‘he performed the action on him’); the verb is past simple. سر از تن جدا is a compact participial-like phrase ‘head from body apart’ meaning ‘he severed the head from the body’ — جدا functions as a predicative/adverbial ‘apart’ and the preposition از marks the source (‘from the body’).
segments.s224-g54: همه کام و اندیشه is a coordinated subject phrase ‘all desire and thought’ (همه = all/entire). شد is the past copula ‘became/were made’. بی‌نوا is a predicate adjective meaning ‘miserable/pitiable/left helpless’ — the clause says that all his desires and thoughts were rendered pitiable/void. This is a succinct, poetic way to say hope and thought were extinguished.
segments.s224-g55: همه is the universal quantifier ‘all’. نامداران is a plural substantive ‘the renowned/illustrious ones’ (famous men). ایران‌زمین is a compound proper noun ‘the land of Iran’ modifying نامداران (i.e., ‘the renowned men of Iran’). The whole segment is a noun phrase (subject).
segments.s224-g56: گرفتند here is an idiomatic past tense meaning ‘they took to/they began’ (literally ‘they took’ but used with بر + (speech) to mean they began uttering something). بر is the preposition marking the target (‘at/to’). پهلوان is ‘the champion/hero’ (indirect object/recipient of the acclamation). آفرین is the exclamation ‘Bravo!’ — so the clause means ‘they began shouting “Bravo!” to the hero.’
segments.s224-g9: کنون‌گر is a poetic conditional 'کنون گر' = 'if now'; بیاید is subjunctive/optative 'may/if he comes' (the 'be-' prefix forms present/subjunctive); به is the preposition linking the verb to the destination phrase; آوردگاه is a compound noun 'place of bringing' used here = 'battlefield/field of battle'. Together this segment sets the protasis: 'If he comes now to the battlefield...'.
segments.s224-g10: نمانم is first‑person negative present/subjunctive or jussive 'I will not remain / I shall not stay'; که introduces the following relative/explanatory clause; ماند is the third‑person form 'remains/is left' describing the person who would otherwise stay; بنزد is a poetic prepositional expression 'at the presence of / near'; سپاه is the object 'army/host'. The clause as a whole gives the consequence: 'I will not remain (namely remain) in the presence of the army.'
segments.s224-g11: بجنبید here carries an intransitive verbal sense 'stirred/moved' (the b‑ prefix is the classical verbal marker); the external agent/subject is left implicit (common in epic narrative) and can be read as the mace or the men; با introduces the instrument/association 'with'; گرز رستم is the instrumental/possessive phrase 'Rostam's mace'; ز جای is the ablative 'from its place'. The phrase describes an immediate motion: 'the mace (or they) moved from its place with Rostam's mace.'
segments.s224-g12: همانگه = 'at that moment / immediately'; برخش is a poetic/elliptical form (here functioning like 'he lifted/raised it' or 'he thrust it out') — an action verb tied to the following compound; اندر is the preposition 'in/into/within'; آورد is the past/heavy-form 'brought' or 'caused to come'; پای is 'foot'. Together: 'immediately he brought his foot (forward)' — a quick, narratively linked motion following the previous stir.
segments.s224-g13: منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' (or 'it is I'); گفت is the past reporting verb 'said' (here used in inverted word order common in Persian: reported clause before the verb); شیراوژن is the speaker's name; و links an epithet or additional name; گردگیر is an epithet 'dust‑clad / wearing a dust‑cloak'. The clause is a quoted self‑identification: 'I am...,' said Shirâvujan, 'the dust‑clad.'
segments.s224-g14: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'; گاهی ... گاه is a balanced pair meaning 'sometimes ... sometimes'; کمند is the noun 'lasso/snare' and افگنم is a verb form meaning 'I cast/throw' (an archaic/poetic present or imperfective first‑person form); تیر is 'arrow'. The clause enumerates the speaker's skill: 'who sometimes casts the lasso, sometimes (casts) the arrow.'
segments.s224-g15: هم‌اکنون = 'right now / at this very moment'; ترا is split into ت (second‑person bound pronoun) + را (definite object marker), marking 'you' as direct object; همچو = 'like/as'; کاموس is a poetic/opaque noun here used in simile (see senses); گرد following it is used as a noun 'dust' (so the compound compares the addressee to a sort of 'Kamus‑dust' or dust‑garment). The segment sets up a simile: 'right now I (will) you like X‑dust...'
segments.s224-g16: بدیده is an adjective 'ill‑fated / ill‑favoured' modifying the impending fate; همی is a frequent poetic particle adding immediacy/emphasis (roughly 'indeed / now'); خاک = 'dust/earth'; باید is the modal 'must / it is necessary'; سپرد is the perfective verb 'to consign / hand over'. Together the clause states the necessity: 'indeed he must be consigned to the dust' (i.e. be struck down or buried).
segments.s224-g17: بدو گفت is a classical word order: بدو (to him) precedes the verb گفت (said), so literally 'to him said.' چنگش is an instrumental/adjunct with the genitive suffix -ش ('his grip/clutch'), indicating he seized or held him while speaking. که introduces an indirect question; نام تو چیست is 'what is your name?' with چیست (چی + ست) the archaic/poetic present copula 'is'.
segments.s224-g18: نژادت کدامست is a direct interrogative: 'your lineage which-is?' where کدامست (کدام + است) functions as 'which is it?'. و links the two questions. کام تو چیست repeats the same indirect-question pattern ('what is your desire?') with the copula split into چی + ست, a poetic variant.
segments.s224-g19: بدان here is an imperative formed from the prefix ب + دان (the entry split in the lexicon); as an imperative it functions like 'tell/let me know.' تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that') and is followed by بدانم, a subjunctive/present meaning 'I may know.' که is the complementizer beginning the rest of the indirect question; روز نبرد = 'day of battle' (genitive relationship).
segments.s224-g20: کرا is an archaic interrogative 'who/whom' used as the object ('whose/whom'). ریختم is the (relict) past of ریختن 'I poured/ I shed' with خون 'blood' as its object. چو begins a temporal clause 'when,' and برخاست گرد = 'the dust/tumult rose' (گرد here poetically = 'dust/tumult').
segments.s224-g21: بدو گفت رستم follows the same classical ordering: بدو 'to him' + گفت 'said' with رستم the speaker (subject). که‌ای is an archaic/poetic exclamatory particle combining که + ای used to introduce direct address; شوربخت is a vocative 'wretched/unfortunate one.'
segments.s224-g22: که here continues the direct speech; هرگز مبادا is a fixed optative/negative formula 'may it never be' (مبادا = 'may it not happen' or 'lest'). گل آن درخت is 'the flower of that tree' (گل = flower, آن = that, درخت = tree) and is the object of the negative wish; the verb completing the wish is omitted here and supplied by the following lines.
segments.s224-g23: کجا is the interrogative 'where/when ever.' چون تو = 'like you' (چون = 'like/as' in comparative sense). در باغ is the locative 'in the garden.' بار آورد is a verbal phrase 'to bear a load/produce fruit' (آورد is the verb 'bring/produce'); together the clause rhetorically asks where a garden-tree has borne fruit like you.
segments.s224-g24: چو here is temporal 'when.' تو is the subject 'you.' میوه اندر شمار آورد is a compact expression 'is brought into the count among the fruits' — میوه 'fruit,' اندر 'in/among,' شمار 'count/number,' and آورد 'brings/places' (here meaning 'to be reckoned among'). The clause explains or amplifies the preceding rhetorical question.

sentences.s224-s13: The spear's tip — and my name — is Death; your head must be washed from your body by the hand.
sentences.s224-s14: At once his grasp came like the wind; two rooks he placed upon the bow.
sentences.s224-s15: The bow, habitually cruel, was like a cloud; it also bore cuirass and was a heathen.
sentences.s224-s16: Rostam lifted his shield to his head when he saw that the arrow would try to pierce the armor.
sentences.s224-s17: He said to him, "Be, O brave rider, for now may your head become sated with battle."
sentences.s224-s18: He fixed his grip on that Pilten and (looked) up at a lofty/dark cypress on the meadow.
sentences.s224-s19: Bad (or ill) was that steed: it would not come beneath a bare bit of the mountain, being weary from hauling.
sentences.s224-s20: In reply his grip said, 'Now flight is better than contending with (one's) own body.'
sentences.s224-s21: He roused that pack‑animal from its place and set his course toward his own army.
sentences.s224-s22: The valiant horseman of fiery mien roused his famed steed from behind.
sentences.s224-s23: Immediately Rostam arrived into their midst; the whole plain was full of their conversation.
sentences.s224-s24: At that moment the impure steed seized his hold; both armies remained amazed at him.
sentences.s224-s1: From that plain he spurred his steed and rode off to bring the news to Azar-gashasp.
sentences.s224-s2: When he came near the Iranians to fight, he drew from his quiver an arrow like a dagger.
sentences.s224-s3: Thus he said: 'This is my place of battle; the heads of the renowned are in my grasp.'
sentences.s224-s4: Where did that man, the catcher of kamus, go—who sometimes cast a lasso, sometimes (shot) an arrow?
sentences.s224-s29: Rostam stood between two ranks, holding a single shining brick in his hand.
sentences.s224-s30: From that sight the Khaghan became deeply sorrowful and was agitated by the turning of wheel and fate.
sentences.s224-s31: To Humān the Chinese Khaghan thus spoke: that time and the earth are tight upon us.
sentences.s224-s32: The famed champion Maran has been assigned to you; send a messenger to make inquiries.
sentences.s224-s33: Hūmān said to him: “I am no anvil (in battle), nor the inner tusk of an elephant.”
sentences.s224-s34: In the world there was no warrior like Kāmos; there was no such fighter and there was no pause/hesitation.
sentences.s224-s35: This rider suddenly seized him with his lasso; do not treat this band as base/without worth.
sentences.s224-s36: May I be unlucky — what will the Creator will? — who will become victorious in this plain of vengeance?
sentences.s224-s25: For a while he kept at it until a grief befell; from above he struck himself to the earth.
sentences.s224-s26: A shred fell from him and he cried “Zanehâr!” (for mercy); Tahmētan threw him straight to the ground.
sentences.s224-s27: At once he severed his head from his body; all desire and thought were left bereft.
sentences.s224-s28: All the renowned men of the land of Iran began crying “Bravo!” to the hero.
sentences.s224-s5: If he comes now to the field of battle, I will not remain near the army.
sentences.s224-s6: Rostam's mace stirred from its place; at that instant he set his foot forward.
sentences.s224-s7: “I am,” said Shirâvujan the dust‑clad, “I sometimes cast the lasso, sometimes the arrow.”
sentences.s224-s8: Even now I must consign you to the dust, like (a) Kamus‑dust (i.e. utterly).
sentences.s224-s9: Seizing him, he said, “What is your name? What is your lineage, and what is your desire?”
sentences.s224-s10: Tell me so that I may know on what day of battle I shed whose blood when the dust (tumult) rose.
sentences.s224-s11: Rostam said to him, “O wretched one! — may the flower of that tree never ...”
sentences.s224-s12: Where has any tree in a garden ever borne such a load as you? When you are counted among the fruits, ...
